exits.label: ◂ back into the lanes of Sigil

npcs.name: Mireille
npcs.title: newly cursed, and refusing to feed
npcs.hail: *(A young woman stands under a guttering lamp with her arms wrapped around herself, and she is shaking — not from cold, you understand at once, because the dead do not feel cold the way you do; she is shaking the way a soul shakes when it is holding a door shut against something on the other side that is stronger than it is. She is beautiful in the wrong way, the new way, the way the recently cursed are: a beauty turned slightly luminous and predatory, skin gone pale and perfect, eyes too bright, a thing the curse polishes onto its new-made even as it hollows them out. And she is starving. You can see it in the tendon-tight stillness of her, in the way her gaze snags on the pulse you do not have and flinches away. For Mireille was, until very recently — weeks, you sense, not years — a living girl, with a name and a mother and a small ordinary life, and then something found her in the dark and made her into this, against her will and without her leave: one of the night-cursed, the blood-drinkers, the things her own grandmother warned her about, now wearing her face. And she has made, in the few weeks since, a decision that is killing her by inches: she will not feed. Not on a person. She would rather starve, rather let the Hunger flay her hollow, than become the monster fully — and the Hunger knows it, and is winning, slowly, and she is so afraid.)* …Don't — *(her voice cracks; she presses back against the lamppost)* …don't come close. Please. I'm — I'm not safe. I'm trying *so hard* to be safe, but I'm so *hungry*, and I don't — I don't trust what I'd do. *(her bright eyes find yours, desperate)* …You're cold. Dead-cold, not — not warm. So maybe it's all right. Maybe you're the one person in this whole strange city I can't *hurt.* …Stay there. …Just — talk to me? …Keep me here, with the talking. …I'm so afraid of what I am when it's quiet.*

npcs.name: The Forgotten
npcs.title: an elder who outlived its own name
npcs.hail: *(Something very old is sitting beneath the violet hangings, so still that you take it at first for a statue, or a heap of fine grey rags — and then it turns its head, slowly, with the awful patience of a thing for which haste stopped meaning anything centuries ago, and you look into a face that is barely a face anymore. Not ruined; worse than ruined: worn smooth. The night-cursed do not age in the body, but something else in them wears away across the long centuries, the way a coin's face wears off in a thousand thousand hands, and this one has been passing through its own endless nights for so unthinkably long that almost everything that made it a someone has been rubbed flat. It is an elder — a methuselah, the deep-old, the kind of night-thing that was already ancient when the youngest gods of your Realm were born — and the Hunger it fed, faithfully, for those uncounted centuries, fed back on it, devouring not its body but its *self*, its memories, its loves, its language, its name, until what remains is mostly appetite wearing the shape of a person out of sheer old habit. And yet it came here. Through the doors. Drawn by the faintest ghost of an instinct it can no longer name, the deep-old thing has walked the City of Doors looking for something — something it has forgotten it is looking for, which is the cruelest kind of searching there is. It regards you with eyes like two holes worn through to the dark behind everything, and when it speaks, the voice comes slow, and dredged, as if each word has to be carried up from the bottom of a very deep and very old well.)* …You. *(a long, dredging pause)* …Warm-that-is-not-warm. Crossed-over. …I am looking. *(slower)* …I have been looking for… …I no longer hold what it is. …But it is here. …Somewhere. …The thing I lost first. …Before I lost the rest.*

npcs.name: Mathias
npcs.title: who kept his humanity across the whole long night
npcs.hail: *(A man stands quietly at the center of the square, and he is one of the night-cursed too — you can see the same pallor, the same stillness, the same old patience — but he wears it utterly differently from the starving girl or the worn-smooth elder, and the difference stops you, because it should not be possible. He looks, simply, like a person. An old soul, yes, with centuries behind the calm grey eyes; but warm, present, *here* — a man who is wholly himself, with the unhurried groundedness of someone who long ago made peace with what he is and what it costs and chose, anyway, to remain. He is the impossible middle term between the other two: as old, nearly, as the worn-smooth elder, and carrying the very same curse that is right now flaying the young woman hollow — and across all those centuries, against the entire weight of the Hunger that ate the elder down to appetite, he never stopped being a man. He inclines his head to you with the small grave courtesy of a soul who has had a very long time to learn good manners and never found a reason to abandon them.)* …Welcome to the Long Midnight, traveler. *(a gentle, unhurried voice)* …Don't mind the company — the starving girl and the old, old quiet one. They're not dangerous to you; nothing here is, to a soul already crossed over. *(his grey eyes hold yours, and there is something steadying in them)* …You've the look of one who's seen a few of these doors already, and is starting to notice the shape under them all. So I'll save you the wondering, and tell you what we three are, plainly: we are the same creature, traveler. The same curse, the same Hunger, the same long night. *(quietly)* …She is what it does to you at the start. The old one is what it does to you at the end, if you let it. …And I am the proof that the end is a *choice*, and not a *sentence.* …Sit with us a while. …There's a thing to be learned here that I think you came a long way to hear.*

variants.text: *(She stays pressed against the lamppost, arms wound tight around herself, and the words come fast and frightened, the way a person talks to keep from listening to something else.)* …Okay. Okay, you stayed. Good. *(a shaky breath she doesn't need)* …I'm Mireille. I was — *(a flinch)* …I *am* Mireille. I keep wanting to say *was*, and that's part of how it gets you, I think, the little surrenders, so I'm not going to. I *am* Mireille. *(she swallows)* …Three weeks ago I was alive. I had a *Tuesday*, three weeks ago — I went to work, I called my mother, I burned the rice, I was going to do better at the rice. And then something found me in the dark on the way home and it — *(her bright eyes go glassy)* …it made me into *this.* Didn't ask. Didn't explain. Just *did* it, and *left* me, and now I'm — I'm one of *them.* The things in the old stories. The blood-drinkers. The night-cursed. *(she presses a hand to her mouth)* …And there's a *Hunger*, and it's not like being hungry, it's like — it's like there's another *me* in here, a starving one, that wants only the one thing, and it's *patient*, and it's *stronger* than I am, and it's just *waiting* for me to get weak enough to let it *drive.* *(barely)* …And I won't feed it. Not on a person. I *won't.* I'd rather — *(she laughs, terrified)* …I'd rather *starve*, except I can't even *die* right, so it's just *worse* and *worse* forever. …Tell me I'm not stupid. …Tell me the starving means something. …Because the Hunger says it doesn't, all night, every night, and I'm running out of ways to argue back.*

nodes.speaker: Mireille
nodes.text: *(She slides down to sit at the foot of the lamppost, hugging her knees, making herself small, keeping the distance between you like a kindness.)* …Ask me things. *(a small, frightened smile)* …Please. The talking helps — it's like, when someone's *asking* me things, I'm a *person* being asked things, and not a — not a *Hunger* in a girl-suit. *(she wraps her arms tighter)* …Just don't ask me to come closer. I'm not safe and I love that you trusted me enough to stay, so let me keep being worth it.

choices.text: *(read the newly cursed — past the Hunger, to the girl holding the door shut against it)* "Let me look at you, Mireille — really look. Not at the Hunger. At you, and what the holding-on is costing."
"Why won't you feed, Mireille? You could take a little, from someone who'd never miss it — most of your kind tell themselves exactly that. What makes you starve instead?"

*(her eyes have gone too bright — the Hunger is surging, right now; hold her here, in the talking, with everything you've got)* "Mireille — look at me. *Me*, not the pulse, not the dark. It's rising right now, I can see it. So we ride it together. You don't have to beat it forever — just stay you for the next ten seconds. Then the next ten. I'm right here, and I'm not going anywhere, and the wave always breaks. Breathe with me, even though neither of us needs to. Stay."

*(the one who knows you are not the hunger you were given, but the thing you choose to feed instead)* "Mireille — listen, because this is the thing the Hunger doesn't want you to work out. You keep asking if the starving means anything. It means *everything*, and here's why. There are two of you in there — the girl and the Hunger — and you talk like it's a war you're losing because the Hunger's stronger. But you've got the rule of it backwards. It's not about *strength.* It's about *feeding.* What you feed grows. What you starve goes quiet. Every night you don't give the Hunger the one thing it wants, it gets a little thinner — and every night you stay *you*, choose *you*, talk to your mother in your head and mean to do better at the rice, *that's* the part you're feeding, and *that's* the part that grows. You're not a girl losing to a monster, Mireille. You're a gardener deciding which thing gets the water. The curse gave you the Hunger — it didn't get to decide what you'd *become*, because what you become isn't the thing that was done to you. It's the thing you feed. And three weeks in, starving, still arguing back — you're feeding the girl. You're *winning*, and you can't see it, because winning this war doesn't look like a victory. It looks exactly like what you're doing right now: staying."
"Hold on a little longer, Mireille. I'll come back — and the girl will still be here when I do, because she's stronger than she thinks."

nodes.speaker: Mireille
nodes.text: *(She goes still and lets you look, and you let your sense move past the bright predatory polish of the curse to the soul holding the line behind it.)* *(And the first thing your sense finds is the war, exactly as she described it but worse than she let on: two presences in one self, the girl and the Hunger, and the Hunger is not a metaphor, it is a real and separate and patient appetite that the curse installed in her like a second nervous system, and it never sleeps, and it never tires, and it whispers the same thing every hour of every night — just a little, just once, from someone who won't miss it, and the pain stops. Your sense reads how reasonable it sounds. How nearly impossible it is to keep refusing a thing that is inside your own skin, that wears your own voice, that offers to end a suffering it is itself causing. And then your sense finds the thing that undoes you: that she is winning. Not the way she thinks winning would look — there is no triumph in her, no strength to spare, she is being flayed hollow and she knows it. But your sense reads the Hunger, and the Hunger is thinner than it was three weeks ago. Starved. Quieted, by inches, every night she refuses it. And your sense reads the girl, and the girl, impossibly, is not thinner. She is feeding on something — on the memory of her mother, on the burned rice and the meant-to-do-better, on the small stubborn insistence I am Mireille, on the very act of refusing — and it is keeping her, barely, alive in the way that matters. Your sense reads the truth she cannot see from inside it: that she has stumbled, terrified and alone and with no one to tell her, onto the one law that governs her whole cursed existence — that the self is not fixed, it is fed, and whichever of the two you feed is the one that grows. She thinks she is losing because it hurts. Your sense reads that the hurt is the sensation of the Hunger starving. She is not losing. She is doing the bravest and most fundamental thing a cursed soul can do: she is choosing, every single night, which wolf to feed — and she is feeding the girl.)*

nodes.speaker: Mireille
nodes.text: *(She rests her chin on her knees and is quiet a moment, and when she answers it is with the particular clarity of someone who has had a great deal of time alone with one question.)* …Why won't I feed. *(softly)* …You're right that I could tell myself the story. *'Just a little. From someone who won't miss it. They'll wake up tired and never know.'* The others *do* tell themselves that — I've met a few, in the dark, and they all have the *speech*, the one about how it's *fine*, how the prey *barely* notices, how you'd be a *fool* to suffer when the answer's right there in every warm body that walks by. *(she shivers)* …And here's the thing, the thing that scares me most: …the speech is *true.* They're *right.* I *could* feed and no one would die and the pain would stop and I could *live* — well. *Exist.* …So it's not that I think they're lying. *(she looks at you)* …It's that I figured out what the speech is *for.* …It's not for the prey. The prey really *would* be fine. …The speech is for *me.* It's the first stone of the road *down.* Because the night you decide a person is a *thing-you-won't-miss-a-little-from* — *(barely)* …that's the night you start being able to *look* at people as *portions.* And I've *seen* where that road goes. The old quiet one, over there, under the hangings? *(she nods toward the Forgotten)* …That's the *end* of the speech. That's a thousand years of *'just a little, they won't miss it.'* …It didn't make them a *monster* all at once. It made them a monster the way water makes a canyon — *patiently*, one *reasonable* sip at a time, until there was nothing left of the *person* who started. *(she wraps her arms tight)* …So I won't take the first sip. …Not because I'm strong. …Because I'm *scared* — scared the right way, of the right thing. …I'd rather starve as *Mireille* …than be *full*, and *comfortable*, and slowly stop being *anyone* at *all.*

nodes.speaker: Mireille
nodes.text: *(You don't try to talk her out of the Hunger — you can't, and pretending you could would only prove you don't understand it. Instead you do the one thing that works: you make yourself an anchor. You hold her gaze and you give her the next ten seconds, and then the next, refusing to let the moment become the forever the Hunger insists it is, and you stay — visibly, stubbornly, unfrightened — so that there is a person in front of her to be a person *for.* And the wave, as you promised, breaks.)* *(She gasps — a sound she doesn't need, pure reflex, the body remembering how it used to cope — and the terrible brightness in her eyes recedes, and she is, shaking and wrecked and *herself*, still herself, on the other side of it.)* …It — *(she presses both hands to her face)* …it broke. It *broke.* Oh — oh, it *always feels like it won't*, that's the *trick* of it, it feels like it'll just *climb* forever until I *break* instead — and you just — you just *sat* there and made me *count*, and it — *(a wild, wet laugh)* …it *broke*, like you *said*, like waves *do*, and I'm *still here.* *(she looks at you, stunned, luminous)* …Do you know what you just *did?* You just — you *proved* it. The thing I keep *losing* faith in at three in the morning. That the Hunger *isn't* a rising tide that *drowns* you. It's a *wave.* It *crests* and it *breaks* and there's an *after*, every time, if you can just — *stay* — *through* the *cresting.* *(barely)* …I've been doing it *alone* for three weeks, white-knuckling every wave with *no* one to count with, *certain* each one was the one I'd lose. …And you just showed me they all *break.* *Every* one. …I can do the next one now. *(fiercely, through tears)* …I can do the next one, because I *know*, now, in my *body* and not just my *head*, that there's an *after* on the far side of it. …*Thank* you. …You didn't *feed* me, and you didn't *fix* me. …You did something *better.* …You *stayed* until the wave *broke*, and now I *believe* in the *after.* …That's — that might be the thing that *saves* me. …The *believing* there's an *after.*

nodes.speaker: Mireille
nodes.text: *(You hold her there with the talking, steady and present, refusing to flinch, and you feel her grip the lifeline of it — the simple fact of a calm voice and a soul that won't leave — and slowly, shaking, she rides the surge instead of being driven by it.)* …Okay. *(a ragged breath)* …Okay. It's — it's passing. It's passing. *(she loosens, by a fraction)* …You stayed steady. That's — that's the thing, when it spikes, I *spiral*, and the spiral's half of it, the *panic* feeds the *Hunger* feeds the *panic* — and you just *didn't* *spiral*, so I had something *level* to hold onto. *(she looks at you, shaken, grateful)* …It didn't break all the way. It's still — it's there, under, it's always there. But you got me *past* the worst of the crest, and that's — God, that's more than I usually manage alone. *(a wobbly breath)* …Come back, would you? *(barely)* …Not because I can't do it myself — I *have* to be able to do it myself, you won't always be here. …But it *helps*, knowing the wave can be *ridden* with someone instead of just *survived* alone. …It makes the next one feel less like *drowning.* …You made it feel *possible.* …Right now *possible* is — *possible* is everything.*

nodes.speaker: Mireille
nodes.text: *(You try to hold her — but you reach for reason, for argument, *you're stronger than it, you've got this*, and reason is exactly the wrong rope; the Hunger is not a debate, and being told to be strong while she is being flayed only makes her feel more alone in it. She squeezes her eyes shut and rides it out herself, barely, and when it passes she is more exhausted than before.)* …Don't — *(a shaky breath)* …don't tell me I'm *strong*, okay? I know you meant it kind. But when it's *cresting* and someone says *'you've got this, you're stronger than it'* — I *don't* feel stronger, I feel like I'm *failing* at being strong, like there's a way I'm *supposed* to be winning this and I'm too *weak* to find it. *(she presses her forehead to her knees)* …It's not a *strength* thing. I *can't* out-*muscle* it; it's *in* my *muscles.* …When it spikes I don't need a *coach*, I need an *anchor* — someone who just *stays*, *level*, and lets me *count* on them being there while the wave does what waves do. *(quietly)* …The *strength* talk makes me *lonelier*, because it means even the person *helping* thinks this is something I should be able to *power* through, and I *can't*, and now I'm *failing* in *front* of them too. *(barely)* …Come back. …And when you do — *(she looks at you)* …don't try to make me *stronger.* …Just be *steady.* …Steady's the thing that actually *holds.*

nodes.speaker: Mireille
nodes.text: *(It goes wrong in the worst way — you step toward her as you speak, an instinct to comfort, closing the distance she has been so desperately guarding — and the Hunger, which has been waiting all night for exactly this, *lunges* behind her eyes. She throws herself backward away from you with a cry that is half her own voice and half something colder, and scrambles around the lamppost, shaking violently, putting iron and space between you.)* …*No* — *(a ragged, doubled voice)* …*don't*, *don't come closer*, I *told* you, I *told* you I'm not — *(she gets the lamppost between you, gripping it white-knuckled)* …you *moved* toward me and it — God, it *surged*, it saw the *closeness* and it *wanted* — *(she squeezes her eyes shut, forcing it down, trembling)* …That's the *thing* I was *trusting* you with. The *distance.* It was the *one* rule, the *one* thing keeping you *safe* and keeping me *me*, and you — you *meant* it *kind*, I *know* you did, but you *crossed* it, and for a *second* there I wasn't — I almost wasn't — *(she opens her eyes, and they are wet and frightened and her own again)* …Please. *Please* don't do that again. *(barely)* …When someone's holding a door shut against something *stronger* than them, you don't *help* by *testing* the *hinges.* …Stay *back.* …*Respect* the *distance.* …It's not me being *dramatic.* …It's the *whole* *fight*, and you almost *cost* me a *round* of it. …Come back. …But come back and *stay* where it's *safe.* …The distance *is* the *kindness.* …*Please* learn that the distance is the kindness.*

nodes.speaker: Mireille
nodes.text: *(She lifts her head from her knees slowly, and as you speak the frantic, flinching fear in her bright eyes is replaced by something she has not let herself feel in three weeks: the possibility that she might be all right. By the end she is staring at you like a drowning swimmer who has just been told the thing under her feet is the bottom.)* …*'You're a gardener deciding which thing gets the water.'* *(barely)* …*'What you feed grows. What you starve goes quiet.'* *(she presses a hand to her chest)* …I've been — oh. *Oh.* I've been thinking about it *completely* *wrong.* This *whole* *time.* *(her voice fills)* …I thought it was a *fight* — me *versus* the Hunger, two *fighters*, and the Hunger's *bigger* and *stronger* and *never* gets *tired*, so I'm *losing*, by *definition*, because the *stronger* fighter *wins*, that's how *fights* *work.* *(a wild, dawning laugh)* …But it's *not* a *fight.* It's a — it's a *garden.* It's *feeding.* And in *that* contest, *strength* doesn't *matter* at all — what matters is *which* one I *water*, and I've been *starving* the Hunger and *feeding* the *girl* for *three weeks* *without* even *knowing* that's what *winning* *looks* *like!* *(she scrambles up, electric)* …That's *why* it *hurts!* The *hurt* isn't me *losing* — the hurt is the *Hunger* *starving!* I've been reading my *own* *victory* as *defeat* because *winning* this *feels* *exactly* *like* *suffering!* *(she laughs, and it breaks into a sob, and she lets it)* …And your *Wall.* *(fiercely, through tears)* …Your Wall looks at *souls* like *me* and sees the *curse* — the *Hunger*, the *monster*, the *thing* that was *done* to us — and writes us *off* as *that*, as *what* *happened* to us. …But what *happened* to me *isn't* what I *am.* …The *curse* is what I was *given.* …*Mireille* is what I'm *feeding.* …*Go* *tell* them, whatever you came here to *learn* — *(she grips the lamppost, blazing)* …tell every soul in that wall that got *written off* for *what* was *done* to them: …*you are not the thing that happened to you. you are the thing you feed.* …And it is *never*, *ever* *too* *late* …to *change* which *one* gets the *water.*

nodes.speaker: Mireille
nodes.text: *(She nods, and wraps her arms around herself again, but there is something steadier in her now, a hand-hold she didn't have before.)* …Okay. *(a small, real smile)* …Go on. Go see the others. *(she glances toward Mathias, and her face fills with a desperate hope)* …That one — the calm one, in the middle — *go* to *him.* *(barely)* …He's like me, you know. *Exactly* like me. Same curse, same Hunger, same — *everything.* …Except he's *old*, and he's *still* *himself*, and I've been *watching* him *be* *okay* across this whole square and not *understanding* *how*, like watching someone *breathe* *underwater.* *(quietly)* …I *need* to know how he did it. How you carry this for *centuries* and come out the *far* end still a *person.* …But I'm — I'm *afraid* to ask him, isn't that stupid? Afraid the answer's something I *can't* do, some *strength* I don't *have.* *(she looks at you)* …*You* ask him. …And then come *back* and *tell* me. …Tell me it's *possible* to be *him.* …Tell me the girl I'm *feeding* …gets to grow up into *that.* …I think I could do *anything* — *survive* anything, *starve* through *anything* — if I just *knew* the far end of it looked like *him.*

variants.text: *(The deep-old thing regards you with its worn-through eyes, and there is no menace in it — menace is a sharp thing, and everything sharp in this creature was rubbed away ages ago — only a vast, slow, patient absence, like a cathedral after the congregation has been gone a thousand years.)* …You stay. *(a dredging pause)* …Most do not. The young one fears me — *(a slow turn of the head toward Mireille)* …she looks at me and sees her ending. She is right to. *(slower)* …I will tell you what I am, warm-cold thing, while I still hold enough words in a row to do it. …I am *old.* I do not know how old. The number wore off. *(a long silence)* …I fed the Hunger. That is all I did, for… for the length of everything. I fed it, and it was *reasonable*, every time — *just a little, they will not miss it* — and the little times were so many that they… they *added.* And the Hunger grew, on all that feeding, the way a fire grows. *(slower still)* …And the *me*, warm-cold thing — the *me* did not eat. I fed the Hunger and I *starved* the *self*, every night, for… for the length of everything. …And a starved thing *shrinks.* My loves went first. Then the faces. Then the words — I had *languages* once, I think, *many* — then the *places*, then the *years*, then… *(the worn eyes search the dark)* …then my *name.* …I came here through the doors because something *pulled* me. The ghost of an *instinct.* I am *looking* for a thing. …I have forgotten *what* the thing is. …I know only that I lost it *first.* …Before I lost *all* the *rest.* …Help me, warm-cold thing. …What does a creature *look* for, …when it has forgotten *everything* …*except* that it is *looking?**

nodes.speaker: The Forgotten
nodes.text: *(It folds its grey hands with the slowness of settling sediment, and waits, with the bottomless patience of a thing for which a moment and a century feel much the same.)* …Ask. *(a dredging pause)* …I will answer if the answer is still… in me. …Much is not. …But you may find that the *asking* — *(slower)* …the asking *stirs* things. …Sometimes a question is a *bucket*, warm-cold thing. …And there is *water*, still, very far down. …I had *forgotten* there was a *bottom* to me at all. …Lower it. …Let us see what comes *up.*

choices.text: *(read the worn-smooth elder — past the vast absence, to whatever of the person is left, however little)* "Hold still, old one. Let me look — all the way down. Past what's worn away, to whatever's left at the bottom."
"What is it you're looking for, old one? You said you lost it first, before all the rest. Let's lower the bucket together — what was the first thing the Hunger took?"
"You said the young girl is right to see her ending in you. Is she? Is this where the starving-the-self road *has* to end — or did you just walk it further than anyone should?"

*(the one who knows the starved self is not the dead self — and that what was starved can be fed again)* "Old one — hear me, because I think you came all this way to be told exactly this. You believe the self is *gone* — worn away, eaten, lost down the centuries one reasonable sip at a time. But it isn't gone. It's *starved.* And those are not the same thing, and the difference is everything. A thing that is gone cannot be recovered. A thing that is *starved* is only *waiting to be fed.* You fed the Hunger for the length of everything and it grew vast; you starved the self for the length of everything and it shrank to a seed — but a seed is not a corpse, old one. It is the most patient living thing there is. The very fact that something *pulled* you here, that an instinct survived when the name didn't, that you are *looking* at all — that's the seed. That's the self, still alive at the bottom of the well, too starved to speak its own name but not too starved to make you *walk a thousand miles toward the memory of being a person.* You are not the ruin of someone. You are someone who was starved nearly to nothing — and 'nearly' is the whole of the hope. Start feeding the other thing. Even now. Even this late. What you feed grows, old one — and it was never too late for the seed, only too late for the regret."
"Rest a moment, old one. I'll come back — and we'll lower the bucket again. There's water down there. I'm sure of it."

nodes.speaker: The Forgotten
nodes.text: *(It holds still — stillness is the one thing it has in endless supply — and you let your sense descend into it, past the vast worn absence, down and down, looking for the bottom of a soul that has been wearing away for the length of everything.)* *(And the descent is the most desolate thing your sense has ever done. Floor after floor of emptiness, rooms where memories used to live, scoured bare: here a space the exact shape of a face, with the face gone; here the cold socket where a language was; here a vast hollow that your sense understands, with a lurch, was once *love* — a specific love, a person, a warmth at the center of a life — worn now to a smooth blank curve, like a step hollowed by ten thousand years of feet. Your sense reads the mechanism of it, and it is the same mechanism the young girl is fighting, only run to its absolute end: every night, for uncounted centuries, this creature fed the Hunger and starved the self, and a starved thing shrinks, and it shrank, and shrank, until almost nothing of the someone remained. Almost. And here, at the very bottom of the well, where your sense had braced to find nothing at all — there is a seed. Tiny. Starved past speech, past memory, past name. But alive. Your sense reads it and knows it instantly for what it is: the irreducible self, the last unworn grain, the thing that even a thousand years of starvation could not quite finish — because, your sense understands, it was never *fed* upon. The Hunger ate the memories and the loves and the name, all the *contents* of the person; but the bare fact of *being a someone at all*, the seed under the contents, the Hunger could not reach, because it is not a content, it is the soil the contents grew in. Your sense reads, last, the most important thing: that the seed is what pulled the creature here. That an instinct too deep to be a memory — *find the way back to being a person* — survived in that grain when everything else wore away, and walked a worn-smooth elder a thousand miles through the doors on the strength of a longing it could no longer name. It is not looking for its name. Your sense reads the truth under the searching: it is looking for *permission to start growing again.*)*

nodes.speaker: The Forgotten
nodes.text: *(It is silent for a very long time — so long you think it has forgotten the question, or you — and then, slowly, the worn eyes change, the way deep water changes when something moves far below the surface.)* …The first thing. *(a dredging pause)* …You ask me to lower the bucket to the *first* thing the Hunger took. …That is — *(slower)* …that is *deep*, warm-cold thing. The first losses are the *lowest.* They have had the *longest* to sink. *(a silence, and then, with terrible effort)* …There was… …a *warmth.* Not a *fire.* A — a *person.* I do not have the *face.* The face wore off… ages past. But I have, still, *very* faint, the — the *shape* of the *feeling.* *(the voice dredges, slow)* …Someone I fed *beside.* No — *(a long pause)* …someone I *did not feed upon.* …That is it. That is the *grain* of it. There was *one* I *would* *not* *feed* *upon.* In all the… the length of everything… there was *one* warmth I held *outside* the Hunger, *exempt*, *safe* — *(the worn voice catches on something it has not touched in an age)* …and *that* — *that* is the thing I lost *first*, warm-cold thing. Not because the Hunger *took* it. …Because *I* *let* it *go.* …There came a night — I cannot hold *which* — when feeding the Hunger was *easier* than *protecting* the *one* warmth, and I… I let the *exemption* *lapse.* I let the *last* *person* I *would* *not* *consume* …become someone I *would.* *(the deepest silence yet)* …And *after* that, warm-cold thing… …after I let *that* go… …there was *nothing* left to *starve* *for.* …The self had *one* *anchor.* …And I *spent* it. …And *everything* *else* …wore *away* …from *there.* …*(the worn eyes find you)* …I have been looking, all this time… …for the *one* I *should* *not* *have* *fed* *upon.* …To tell them… …I am *sorry.* …It is the *only* word …that did not …wear *off.**

nodes.speaker: The Forgotten
nodes.text: *(The worn head turns, slowly, toward the starving girl across the square, and regards her for a long, long moment, and something that might once have been tenderness moves through the vast absence like wind over a dead sea.)* …Is she right. *(a dredging pause)* …That I am her *ending.* *(slower)* …She is right that I am *an* ending. …She is *wrong*, warm-cold thing, that I am the *only* one. …That is the thing I would tell her, if I could hold enough words together to *cross* the *square* and *say* it. *(a silence)* …I did not arrive *here* because the road *leads* here. …I arrived here because *I* *walked* *it* *all* *the* *way* *down.* …Every night, the *choice* came — *feed the Hunger, or feed the self* — and every night, for the length of everything, I chose… *(the worn voice slows)* …the *easy* one. The *reasonable* one. *Just* *a* *little.* …And the *easy* choice, made *ten* *thousand* *times*, …*becomes* *a* *road*, …and the *road* *leads* *here.* *(the eyes find you)* …But it was *never* *one* *road*, warm-cold thing. …It was *ten* *thousand* *separate* *nights*, and on *any* *one* of them — *any* — I could have fed the *self* *instead*, and *been*, that night, a *little* *more* *someone* …instead of a little *less.* *(the deepest, slowest grief)* …Tell her — the *young* one — …tell her I am *not* her *fate.* …I am her *warning.* …A *fate* is a thing that *happens* *to* you. …I am a thing I *did*, *one* *easy* *night* at a *time.* …She is doing the *hard* thing, *now*, *new*, while it still *costs* her *little* to *choose.* …If she *keeps* *choosing* it… …she will *never* …*never* …become *me.* …I am not the *end* of her *road.* …I am the end of the *other* one. …The one she is, *every* *night*, *bravely*, *refusing* to *walk.**

nodes.speaker: The Forgotten
nodes.text: *(For a long while it does not respond at all, and you wonder if the words reached anything. And then — slowly, the way the first crack travels through ice in spring — something *moves* at the bottom of the vast worn dark, and the creature draws a breath it has not needed in ten thousand years, and when the dredged voice comes it is, for the first time, not entirely empty.)* …*'Starved is not gone.'* *(a long, trembling pause)* …*'A seed is not a corpse.'* *(the worn eyes lift to you, and far, far down in them, something flickers that has not flickered in an age)* …I had — warm-cold thing, I had *grieved* the self as *dead.* That was — that was the *peace* I had made, the only one left to me: that the *person* was *gone*, *worn* *away*, *unrecoverable*, and so there was *nothing* to do but *carry* the *absence* and *wait* for the *doors* to *run* *out.* *(the voice catches)* …And you say — you say it is not *gone.* It is *starved.* And a *starved* thing… *(barely)* …a starved thing is only *waiting* to be *fed.* *(something is happening in the vast absence — not a filling, not yet, but a *turning*, a thing orienting itself toward a light for the first time in centuries)* …You are *right* that something *pulled* me *here.* …I called it an *instinct.* …You call it the *seed.* …The *self*, still *alive* at the *bottom* of the *well*, too *starved* to *speak* its *name* but not too starved to make me *walk* a *thousand* *miles* *toward* the *memory* of being *someone.* *(the worn voice fills, impossibly, with the faintest tincture of *hope*, a thing it had worn entirely away)* …Then I have been *feeding* the *wrong* *thing* …for the *length* of *everything*. …And I can — *(barely)* …I can *stop.* …*Tonight.* …I can lower the bucket to the *seed* instead of the *Hunger*, and *water* *that*, and — *(the eyes find you, and for one moment the worn-smooth face is almost, almost a face again)* …and your *Wall*, warm-cold thing. …It would look at *me* — the *ruin*, the *worn-smooth* *husk*, the *thing* with *nothing* *left* — and call me *spent.* *Finished.* *Owed* *nothing.* …But you have *shown* me the *Wall* *cannot* *see* the *seed.* …*No* *power* can. …It is *too* *small*, and *too* *deep*, and *too* *alive.* …*Go*, warm-cold thing. …Tell the *worn-away* *souls* in that *wall* — the ones *everyone*, *even* *themselves*, has *given* *up* on: …*you were not eaten. you were starved. and the seed is still there.* …*Start* *watering* *it.* …It is the *one* *thing* …a thousand years of *ruin* …could *never* *quite* *finish.**

nodes.speaker: The Forgotten
nodes.text: *(It inclines its head, the gesture slow as a glacier, and there is in the vast worn quiet of it something fractionally less empty than when you came.)* …Go. *(a dredging pause)* …And — warm-cold thing. *(slower)* …You *lowered* the *bucket.* …I *felt* it *touch* *water.* …I had *forgotten* …there was *water.* *(the worn eyes drift toward Mathias, at the center of the square)* …The *steady* one. …Go to *him.* …He is what I *might* have been, warm-cold thing… …had I *fed* the *self* on the *nights* I *fed* the *Hunger.* …Same *curse.* Same *length* of *nights.* …He *watered* the *seed* …and *I* watered the *Hunger* …and *that* — *(the slow voice holds a grief and, under it, something almost like wonder)* …*that* is the *only* difference between *us.* …Not *strength.* …Not *luck.* …*Which* *thing* we *fed.* …He will *tell* you *how* he *did* it. …And you must *carry* it to the *young* one, who *still* *has* *all* her *nights* *ahead.* *(barely)* …I cannot get my *nights* *back.* …But she has *hers.* …And *he* is the *proof* …of *where* they can *lead.* …*Go.* …And — *(the worn voice, very faint)* …*thank* you …for *believing* there was a *bottom* …when even *I* had *stopped.**

variants.text: *(The steady man watches you with his calm grey eyes, and there is in his bearing none of the girl's terror nor the elder's absence — only a deep, worn, hard-won peace, the peace of a soul that has carried an enormous weight for an enormous time and learned exactly how to hold it so it does not crush him.)* …You've spoken to them both, I'd guess. *(a gentle nod)* …The girl, and the old, old one. The start of the curse, and the end of it. *(quietly)* …And now you've come to the one who doesn't fit the pattern, to ask the obvious question: how. *(a small, grave smile)* …I'll tell you, traveler, because it's the whole reason I stay in this square instead of moving on — someone has to be the standing proof that the middle road exists, or the young ones look at the old ones and despair. *(he folds his hands)* …I am as old as that worn-smooth elder, near enough. Same curse. Same Hunger — and make no mistake, traveler, I feel it; it never left me, it never *quiets* entirely, it has whispered the same reasonable poison in my ear every night for centuries. *(steadily)* …The difference between the elder and me is not that I am stronger, or better, or more loved by any power. There is no power involved. *(he meets your eyes)* …The difference is that on the nights the choice came — feed the Hunger, or feed the man — the elder, mostly, fed the Hunger, and I, mostly, fed the man. That is all. That is the entire secret. There is no trick under it, no grace, no special soul. …Just ten thousand small choices, each one easy to get wrong, and a single rule I made very early and never once let myself break. …Ask me, traveler. …I have watched a great many young ones starve for want of someone telling them plainly that the far end of this can look like a person. …Let me be that, for you to carry back to her.*

nodes.speaker: Mathias
nodes.text: *(He settles, unhurried, with the groundedness of a soul entirely at home in itself, and waits with the easy attention of a man who has learned that listening well is most of kindness.)* …Ask freely. *(a calm smile)* …I've no armor to get past, traveler, and nothing I'd hide. A soul that spent centuries learning to stay honest with *itself* doesn't have much trouble being honest with a stranger. …What would you know?

choices.text: *(read the one who stayed human — past the calm, to the cost and the discipline that bought it)* "Let me look at you, Mathias — at how it's really held. Centuries of that Hunger, and still a man. I'd see what that actually took."
"How, Mathias? Plainly. Centuries of the same Hunger that hollowed the elder, and you're still you. What's the rule you never broke?"
"You said you made one rule, very early. What was it? The single line you'd never cross, that held the man together across the whole long night?"

*(the one who knows a soul is not its curse, but the thing it cultivates against it)* "Mathias — you say there's no trick, no special soul, just ten thousand small choices and a rule. I think you're underselling the most important thing a cursed soul can know, so let me say it back to you the way the whole of these doors has taught me to. The girl thinks she's losing a fight. The elder thought he was a ruin. They both believed the curse *decides* — that what was done to you is what you become. You're the living disproof, and you're so used to it you've stopped seeing how radical it is. The Hunger is what was *done* to you. The man is what you *fed.* And across centuries you proved the thing every soul mortared in my Realm's Wall needs to hear: that a soul is *not* the worst thing that ever happened to it, *not* the curse it was handed, *not* the appetite installed in its skin against its will. A soul is the thing it cultivates, night after night, in the teeth of all that. You didn't survive the curse, Mathias. You *out-grew* it — fed the man so faithfully, so long, that the man is simply *bigger* now than the Hunger ever got to be. That's not the absence of a trick. That's the only trick there is. And it's available to every single soul that ever despaired of being anything but what was done to it."
"Stay steady, Mathias — the girl needs you in this square. I'll come back, and I'll carry what you've said to her."

nodes.speaker: Mathias
nodes.text: *(He inclines his head and lets you look, open and unguarded, and you let your sense move through the calm to find what it is really made of.)* *(And the first thing your sense finds is that the calm is not the absence of the Hunger — it is the *mastery* of it, which is a far costlier and more impressive thing. The Hunger is there, exactly as it is in the girl and was in the elder: the same separate, patient, reasonable appetite, whispering the same poison. It never left. Your sense reads that Mathias has felt it every single night for centuries and has simply, every single night, *not fed it the thing it wants* — and your sense reads what that has cost, the sheer accumulated weight of it, ten thousand nights of saying no to a thing inside his own skin, and understands that the peace in his grey eyes is not the peace of someone for whom this is easy. It is the peace of someone for whom it has never once been easy and who does it anyway, so reliably, for so long, that the doing has become who he is. And your sense finds the rule — the single early vow he built everything on — and reads how it works: not as a cage, but as a *root.* A line he would not cross, fixed so deep and so early that on the nights the Hunger was strongest and his own resolve was weakest, the rule held even when *he* couldn't, the way a tree's roots hold it through a storm the trunk alone could never withstand. Your sense reads that this is the secret the elder missed: not strength of will, which fails, which *everyone's* fails — but a structure built *outside* the will, a vow placed where the Hunger couldn't argue with it, so that the man did not have to win the fight fresh every night; he only had to not dig up the root. And your sense reads, last, the thing that moves you most: that Mathias is not free of the curse, and never will be, and knows it, and has made his peace not with *winning* but with *fighting* — that he has accepted he will feel the Hunger every night until the world ends, and has decided that this is *fine*, that the fighting is not a burden to be escaped but simply the shape of his particular life, the way some souls garden and some build and he, for centuries, has tended the man against the monster, and found, in the tending itself, something that is very nearly joy.)*

nodes.speaker: Mathias
nodes.text: *(He's quiet a moment, ordering it, wanting to get it right because he knows it will be carried to someone who needs it.)* …How. *(a calm breath)* …Plainly, then, the way I promised. …First: I stopped trying to *win.* *(he sees your look, nods)* …That sounds like surrender. It's the opposite. The young ones — the girl — they fight the Hunger like it's a *war* they can *end*, a thing they can *defeat* and finally be *free* of. And when it comes back the next night, as it always does, they feel like they *lost*, and the losing is what breaks them, not the Hunger. *(steadily)* …I gave that up early. I accepted that I will feel the Hunger *every night until the world ends*, and that this is simply *true*, and not a *failure.* Once I stopped needing to *win*, traveler, I only had to get through *tonight* — and *tonight* is always *possible*, even when *forever* is not. *(quietly)* …Second: I built the rule *outside* myself. I'll tell you what it was, if you ask it directly. But the *point* of it is that I did not trust my *will* — my will is as weak as anyone's, weaker, some nights. So I made one fixed line so *early*, and held it so *consistently*, that it stopped being a *choice* I had to *win* and became simply a thing *I do not do*, the way you don't claw your own eyes however much they itch. *(he meets your gaze)* …And third — the one that matters most, traveler. …I kept one *tie* to the warm world. Always. One person, in each mortal generation, that I *loved* and *would not feed upon* and *let myself need.* …Not for *their* sake, though it served them. For *mine.* *(softly)* …Because a creature with *one* thing it will not consume, traveler, *one* warmth it holds *exempt* — that creature still has a *shape.* A *center.* A *self* organized around the *not-consuming.* …The elder lost itself because it let the *last* exemption *lapse*, and after that there was nothing to be a *self* *around.* …I never let mine lapse. *(quietly)* …In four hundred years I have loved, and buried, and loved again, and it has *hurt* every time, the way it hurt the silver-haired woman in the garden door if you've met her. …But the *hurting*, traveler — the *hurting* is the *Hunger's* one *defeat.* …Every grief is *proof* I still had something I *would* *not* *eat.* …*That* is how. …I stayed *human* by always keeping *one* warm thing I *refused* to let the curse *make* *cold.*

nodes.speaker: Mathias
nodes.text: *(He nods slowly, and for the first time a shadow of the centuries crosses the calm grey eyes — not a breaking of the peace, but the weight under it briefly showing through.)* …The rule. *(quietly)* …It was this, traveler, and I made it the first year, while I was still close enough to the living man I'd been to know what I most feared losing. …*I will never feed upon the unwilling, and I will never feed to the death, and I will never, ever, let myself decide that a person is a portion.* *(steadily)* …Three lines, but they're one rule, really. The *first sip* the girl is so wise to fear — the moment you let yourself look at a warm soul and see *food* instead of a *someone* — that is the line. And I drew it, traveler, *before* I was desperate enough to want to cross it, which is the only time you *can* draw it honestly. *(a breath)* …I have fed, across the centuries. A creature must; I made my peace with *that* too. But always from the *willing* — and there are more willing than you'd think, traveler, souls who'll give a little for coin or for the strange comfort of it — and always with the *person* kept firmly in view, never reduced to a *meal.* *(he meets your eyes)* …Do you see the genius of it, traveler? It is not *my* genius — it is just the shape of the thing. The rule does not depend on my being *strong* on any given night. It is *fixed*, *outside* the night's weakness, *decided* by a *better* version of me in a *calmer* hour, *standing guard* over all the worse versions that came after. *(quietly)* …The elder had no such rule, or let it lapse, and so had to win the fight *fresh*, with *willpower alone*, *every* night — and willpower *alone*, across *ten thousand nights*, *will* fail, traveler, on the law of averages if nothing else. *Everyone's* does. *(softly)* …Mine never had to. …Because I did not leave the man's survival to the man's *strength.* …I left it to a *root* I planted *early*, and *watered* *daily*, and *never*, *whatever* I felt, *dug* *up.* …*That* is the vow. …Carry it to the girl, traveler. …Tell her: *don't fight the Hunger with willpower. Outbuild it with a rule.* …Willpower is the *trunk.* …A vow kept *daily* is the *root.* …And it is the *root* that holds you …through the *storms* the *trunk* *cannot.**

nodes.speaker: Mathias
nodes.text: *(He listens to the whole of it without moving, the calm grey eyes steady on yours, and when you finish he is quiet for a long moment, and then he does something you do not expect: he laughs, softly, and there is wonder in it, and the wetness of an old emotion long mastered but never gone.)* …*'You out-grew it.'* *(quietly)* …Traveler. In four hundred years, do you know, no one has ever put it to me quite like that. *(he presses his hands together)* …I have always said *survived.* *Endured.* *Held the line.* All the *defensive* words — as though the best I'd managed was to *keep* the Hunger from *taking* the man. *(his voice fills)* …But you're right. That's not what happened, is it. A thing you merely *hold off* stays the *same size* forever, and you stay *forever* its *jailer*, exhausted, one bad night from ruin. …That is not my life. *(steadily, with dawning clarity)* …What *happened*, across those centuries, is that I *fed* the *man* — every night, every grief, every kept vow, every warm thing I refused to let go cold — and *starved* the *Hunger*, and so the *man* *grew*, and the *Hunger* *thinned*, until — *(he looks at his own hands as if seeing them new)* …until the man is simply *bigger* now than the Hunger ever managed to *become.* I am not its *jailer*, traveler. I *out-grew* it, the way an oak out-grows the stake it was tied to as a sapling. *(and the calm returns, but deeper now, lit from under)* …And your *Wall.* *(quietly, with the steadiness of total conviction)* …Your Wall would price *all three* of us by the *curse* — *monsters*, *blood-drinkers*, *the* *thing* that was *done* to us — and *mortar* us *in* as *that.* …But the curse is the *least* *true* thing about any of us, traveler. It is the *one* thing we *did* *not* *choose.* …*Go* and tell every soul in that wall the thing you just told *me*, that I was *too* *close* to *see*: …*you are not the worst thing that was done to you. you are the thing you have fed, night after night, in the teeth of it.* …The girl is *feeding* the *man* and *cannot* *see* she's *winning.* …The elder *fed* the *Hunger* and *thinks* he's *finished* — but even *he* has a *seed* left to *water.* …And *I* am *only* what *any* of them *becomes*, traveler, *given* the *one* truth your *whole* journey is *for*: …that a *soul* is not its *curse. …It* is its *cultivation.* …*Go.* …And *carry* the *girl* the *proof*, *standing* *here*, that the *far* *end* of the *long* *night* …can *look* …like a *man.**

nodes.speaker: Mathias
nodes.text: *(He nods, and the calm grey eyes hold a warmth that four centuries have deepened rather than worn.)* …Go well, traveler. *(a small, grave smile)* …And thank you — for *asking* the *how*, instead of just *marveling* at the *that.* The marveling does the young ones no good; it makes me a *miracle*, and a miracle can't be *copied.* The *how* can. *(he glances toward Mireille, and his calm fills with a quiet, fatherly tenderness)* …Carry it to her. *Plainly*, the way I gave it to you — no grand speech, she's had enough grand speeches from the ones who feed and call it freedom. *(quietly)* …Tell her three things, and tell her I said them, so she knows they come from a soul that *walked* the whole long night and came out *human.* …*One:* stop trying to *win* — just get through *tonight*, because *tonight* is always *possible.* …*Two:* don't trust your *willpower* — build a *rule*, *early*, and let the *root* hold you when the *trunk* *can't.* …*Three* — *(and here his steady voice goes very gentle)* …tell her to *keep* *one* *warm* *thing* she will *never* let the *curse* make *cold.* …*One.* …That's *all* it *takes* to *have* a *shape.* …And tell her — *(he looks at the girl across the square, starving and stubborn and three weeks into forever)* …tell her the *man* in the *middle* of the *square* *started* *exactly* *where* *she* *is.* …*Terrified.* *Starving.* *Certain* he'd *lose.* …And *four* *hundred* *years* *later* …he is *still* *here* …and *still* *himself.* …She *can* *become* *me*, traveler. …*Go* and *tell* her so. …It is, I think, the *one* thing she *needs* *more* than *blood.*
